channel.title: Policy on After the Code
channel.description: Recent content in Policy on After the Code

item.title: A Critical Reading of Structural Ethics in Cybersecurity Policy: Korea's 2025 Whole-of-Government Information Protection Plan
item.description: A reading of Korea's 2025 whole-of-government information protection plan through the structural parallel between the Nightingale myth and the white-hacker discourse. Policy is moving from dependence on individual ethics toward structural accountability, but the transition is not complete.

item.title: The Moment AI Truly Becomes New: Not When It Finds the Answer, but When It Rewrites the Problem
item.description: Through the Nightingale myth, the white-hacker discourse, the Sterbenz lemma, and browser exploit reasoning, this essay argues that the real change LLMs bring lies not in knowledge retrieval but in problem reframing.

item.title: Can the Market Move Governance?
item.description: Policy is not the only thing that creates change. Once external actors — insurers, customers, supply chains, evaluation services, security SaaS — start pricing the cost, governance eventually follows.

item.title: How Do We Measure Adaptive Capability?
item.description: To move from compliance capability to adaptive capability, what do we measure? This post proposes MTTA, MTTP, MTRS, and a minimal execution template for the field.

item.title: Why Korean Security Governance Does Not Change
item.description: A game-theoretic analysis of why Korean security governance stays stuck when NIS, KISA, the Board of Audit, the security industry, CISOs, and policy agencies are each acting rationally.

item.title: Beyond CVE Response: AI-Era Vulnerabilities Move Before They Get Numbers
item.description: AI-era vulnerability response cannot wait for a CVE number. Pre-CVE signals such as issues, commits, PoCs, write-ups, and patch traces now have to be mapped against internal exposure earlier.

item.title: Korean Security Governance Is Accelerating in the Wrong Direction in the AI Era
item.description: Korean security governance in the AI era needs to change not the title of any one agency, but the behavior that evaluation rewards.

item.title: eIDAS 2.0 vs. Korea’s Digital Identity System: A Comparative Analysis
item.description: Comparative analysis of EU eIDAS 2.0 wallet-based identity and Korea's mobile ID system across governance, privacy, and operations.

item.title: Is Your Data in the Cat's Paws?
item.description: Analysis of the 2025 KakaoPay breach exposing 40M users' data, and why formal consent fails without AI-based DPIA and civic oversight.

item.title: There’s No Such Thing as a Free Lunch, But Security Was Free
item.description: The CVE system nearly collapsed in 2025. Who should fund public cybersecurity infrastructure when free-riding is no longer sustainable?
